Even if we go against the google grain,  there would need to be a way to express the 1 point bounds concept.  If we take (x,x) to mean wrapping around the entire world, then we need to come up something for the other concept.

It's not the greatest idea to return non-ASCII things in Py2 because who wants to deal with possible terminal issues when they want to just debug some stuff, but it "works" / "is allowed" if someone decides to. It's less important I think to actually support non-ASCII things in these places ("nice" as that might be) as it is to make sure not to start *mixing in* implicit conversion for someone who might encounter the return values and then start seeing unicode errors even though they're doing things "correctly" -- but you're definitely right that this is tiny-percentage nonsense, I just sort of covered a few of 'em at once.

I think this don't respect the pep8 standard:

Yes
```
# Aligned with opening delimiter.
foo = long_function_name(var_one, var_two,
                         var_three, var_four)
```

No:
```
# Arguments on first line forbidden when not using vertical alignment.
foo = long_function_name(var_one, var_two,
    var_three, var_four)
```

I think it makes more sense for this to be `TypeError`.

`AttributeError` would imply that when doing `foo.bar`, the attribute `bar` couldn't be found. But what is happening here is that `foo.bar` was found fine, but `foo.bar(...)` had an error.

Also, `TypeError` matches what happens for Python builtins and stdlib:

```

----> 1 list.append(5)

TypeError: descriptor 'append' requires a 'list' object but received a 'int'

I think this is a must have functionality, especially now that we're doing a lot of improvements for our distributed tracing compatibility. What I suggest here is having an approach similar to what we already have in our [aiohttp integration][1]. Mostly, it would be great if:
* we make distributed tracing configurable, so another kwarg [in the middleware][2] (let's say: `distributed_tracing=False`). In that way, distributed tracing can be enabled/disabled, and by default is disabled (most conservative approach)
* make it possible to set independently the `trace_id` and the `parent_id`. This is a wanted functionality, because when you're moving a trace from service A to service B, you want both fields. But in cases where the `trace_id` is (for instance) the request ID chosen by HAProxy, you want to create the root span of your trace with such ID.

Makes sense to you these changes? I think they improve your current implementation, while providing the same functionality you need. Thanks a lot for the contribution!


[1]: https://github.com/BrendanBall/dd-trace-py/blob/69f5fed4ac1cdb56a75636d2ad3a626cdd187f70/ddtrace/contrib/aiohttp/middlewares.py#L39-L48
[2]: https://github.com/BrendanBall/dd-trace-py/blob/69f5fed4ac1cdb56a75636d2ad3a626cdd187f70/ddtrace/contrib/flask/middleware.py#L25

Okay, so from what I've uncovered so far `ddtrace.__init__.py` is fine for manual usage. Issues arise when tracing via the `ddtrace-run` command. When `ddtrace-run` executes, `ddtrace.__init__.py` is invoked in `sitecustomize.py` which doesn't give us any useful application or file name.

I'm going to continue digging further into this.

What's the reasoning behind making the response handler `api.echo_handler` here?

- Keep the spinel property definitions as is (but change the documentation to avoid confustion)
- Map two of spinel properties `ONLINE` and `DEEP_SLEEP`  to `ON` and `SLEEP` on the `otPlatPowerState` platform APIs (fail on other values).

> So what happens if it's heap-allocated and not destroyed at the end of the scope? Is it meant to never be created not on the stack?

It's basically just an RIAA-type class that overrides the globals while it exists then restores them when it gets destroyed.  So if it never gets destroyed, its override never gets restored (but this is basically true of any RAII class).  So I think the answer to the second question is "yes, usually", but I don't see that it would break if you managed it yourself on the heap (the worst case is that the values from before you instantiated it will never be restored).

What @beardypig meant was that usually when you're matching (optional) strings, you should try to match the same kind of quotation mark at the end of the string which was used at the beginning of it. The regex should look something like that:
```python
r"""channel_id\s*=\s*(?P<quote>['"]?)(?P<value>\d+)(?P=quote)"""
```
This also replaces the unnecessary `\s*?` with a simple `\s*` and gives each capture group a name, as it increments the position of the value capture group by one. This needs to get fixed in the code below...
